coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Jing
Song, Na
Zhang, Ya-Kun
Zhong, Shu-Xian
Wang, Ai-Jun
Chen, Jianrong
coredata.dc:description: Green, low-cost, and water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots were prepared under hydrothermal conditions, with a plant of Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source. The as-synthesized C-dots have better stability and relatively high photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 50.78%, along with the fluorescence lifetime of ca. 3.84ns. Their photoluminescence can be significantly quenched by simply using the buffer solution of HAC–NaAC for Hg2+ and Tris–HCl for Fe3+. Based on this, we explored for selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ with high selectivity, fast response, low cost, and broad linear ranges of 0.01–100μM for Hg2+ and 0.025–100μM for Fe3+, as well as low detection limits of 5.5nM (Hg2+) and 0.075μM (Fe3+) (S/N=3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Jinhua bergamot
Fluorescence
Metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
Fluorescence detection for Hg2+ and Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of C-dots. Inset shows C-dots under visible light (right) and 365nm UV light (left). (B) The corresponding emission spectra of C-dots at different excitation wavelength from 290 to 390nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Survey (A), high-resolution C1s (B), O1s (C), and N1s (D) XPS spectra of C-dots.
The fluorescence intensities of C-dots at 440nm (excitation at 330nm) with different pH values (A) and time (B).

The difference in relative PL intensities of C-dots between the blank and solutions containing different 0.5mM metal ions (excitation at 330nm, F and F 0 are PL intensities of C-dots in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively).
The fluorescence spectra of C-dots with different concentrations of Hg2+ (A) and Fe3+ (B) from 0 to 50μM, respectively.
The fluorescence intensities of C-dots in Tris-HCl buffer and in water containing 0.5mM Hg2+ and Fe3+, respectively.
Plots of the (F 0 − F)/F 0 with different concentrations of Hg2+ (A) and Fe3+ (B).

The relationship between the F/F 0 and Hg2+ concentrations (A: 0.01–0.1μM; B: 1–100μM) by fitting to the Stern–Volmer equation. The relationship between the F/F 0 and Fe3+ concentrations (C: 0.025–1μM; D: 5–100μM) by fitting to the Stern–Volmer equation.
Comparison of different fluorescent C-dots for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geography and Environmental Sciences, College of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Geography and Environmental Sciences, College of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An environmental-friendly and low-cost hydrothermal method was developed for synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent C-dots with Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source. The fluorescence of the C-dots can be served as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ or Fe3+ with high sensitivity and selectivity, respectively.

Green, low-cost, and water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots were prepared under hydrothermal conditions, with a plant of Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source. The as-synthesized C-dots have better stability and relatively high photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 50.78%, along with the fluorescence lifetime of ca. 3.84ns. Their photoluminescence can be significantly quenched by simply using the buffer solution of HAC–NaAC for Hg2+ and Tris–HCl for Fe3+. Based on this, we explored for selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ with high selectivity, fast response, low cost, and broad linear ranges of 0.01–100μM for Hg2+ and 0.025–100μM for Fe3+, as well as low detection limits of 5.5nM (Hg2+) and 0.075μM (Fe3+) (S/N=3).

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions induce many environmental issues, and thereby a lot of efforts have been focused on their monitor and detection, especially in plant, soil, and waste water [1,2]. In the past two decades, quantum dots have attracted much attention for fluorescent detection of heavy metal ions [3–6], owing to their good optical properties and biocompatibility. As a result, a variety of methods have been developed, such as organic method [7], aqueous solution method [8], and hydrothermal method [9]. However, the above methods are complex and expensive, along with serious toxicity of the as-prepared quantum dots to surrounded environment and animals’ health. Therefore, it is essential to develop a simple method for synthesis of cheap and biocompatible nanomaterials.

Alternatively, carbon dots (C-dots), as a new type of emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, have high fluorescence photostability and up-converted photoluminescence, as well as their wide potential and/or practical applications in new energy fields [10], bio-imaging [11,12], optoelectronic devices [13], biosensors [14], medical diagnosis [15,16], catalysis [17], and metal cations analysis [18–22]. Moreover, C-dots have all the advantages of traditional semiconductor quantum dots, such as superior optical property, low toxicity, good chemical stability and biocompatibility, along with facile synthesis.

More importantly, biomass is widely used as carbon sources for synthesis of C-dots. C-dots were prepared by a hydrothermal method for the detection of Hg2+ [18], utilizing the rhizome of Giant Knotweed Rhizome (a traditional Chinese medicine) as a carbon source. C-dots were obtained under hydrothermal conditions for Hg2+ determination in lake water samples [23], using the wastes of pomelo peels as carbon sources. High fluorescence C-dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method for the detection of Fe3+, using citric acid as carbon sources [21]. The sensing principle can be explained by the fact that the fluorescence of C-dots is easy to be quenched in the presence of metal ions, presumably owing to facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation via an effective electron or energy transfer process [10].

In this paper, an environmental-friendly and low-cost hydrothermal method was developed for preparation of water-soluble fluorescent C-dots with Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source. The fluorescence properties of the as-prepared C-dots were examined by fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ as model systems.

Jinhua bergamot was bought from the local supermarket and washed several times with water before use. FeCl3, CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2, CoCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, MnSO4,CuCl2, Al2(SO4)3, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl2, Cr(NO3)2, AgNO3, sodium acetate, and acetic acid were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hg(NO3)2 were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). All the other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. The water used here was purified through a Millipore System throughout the whole experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 950 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a LS-45 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, UK). The fluorescence lifetime was measured on an Edinburgh FLS 920 photocounting system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a thermoelectron instrument (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) with Al Kα X-ray radiation as the source for excitation (1486.8eV, 500μm). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope using an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) experiments were conducted on a Nicolet 670 FTIR spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets.

For typical preparation of fluorescent C-dots, 0.5g of bergamot was dispersed into 10mL water under stirring. Afterward, the mixture was transferred into a 15mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave, heated at 200°C for 5h, and then cooled to room temperature naturally. The yellow fluorescent C-dots suspension was collected by removing the large dots with 0.22μM filter membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 20μL of 10-fold diluted suspension of C-dots was put into 0.2mL of HAC–NaAC solution (pH 7.0), followed by the addition of different Hg2+ concentrations, and finally diluted to 1mL. The as-prepared suspension was left to react for 4min at room temperature, and then recorded the associated fluorescence spectra accordingly. Similar procedures were employed for recording the fluorescence spectra by varying the Fe3+ concentrations in Tris–HCl solution (pH 7.4), rather than HAC–NaAC solution (pH 7.0), while other conditions were kept constant.

The selectivity of C-dots was examined by the interfered metal ions such as Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Fe2+ under the identical conditions. According to the experiments, the quenching efficiency of C-dots with Hg2+ and Fe3+ is fitted to Stern–Volmer equation, F 0/F =1+ K SV[Q].

Water-soluble fluorescent C-dots were simply prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, using Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source. The as-prepared suspension is yellow under visible light (inset in Fig. 1A), while it is blue under UV light (365nm), revealing their blue photoluminescence property. As shown in Fig. 1A, the maximum emission appears at 440nm when the excitation wavelength is 330nm. It is noticed that the emission spectra are closely correlated with the excitation wavelengths from 290 to 390nm, accompanied with gradually red shift of the emission peaks (Fig. 1B).

The structures and compositions of C-dots can also be identified by XPS experiments. The method of point peak about XPS spectrum of C-dots is based on the software of XPS PEAK4.1. As shown in Fig. 4A, the XPS spectrum of C-dots displays three typical strong peaks at 285.0, 399.1, and 530.1eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. It means that C-dots mainly contain C (44.30%), O (36.00%), and N (19.70%) elements. The amount of N (19.70%) is higher than that of carbon particles in the literature (6.88%, 9.15%) [24,25]. This is attributed to self-surface passivation of carbon particles.

As seen from the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4B), there are four peaks located at 283.7, 284.4, 285.6, and 287.3eV, which are originated from the CC, COH, CN, and CO groups, respectively [24,26]. Meanwhile, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4C) shows two representative peaks at 530eV and 530.7eV, which are come from the COH/COC and CO bands [23]. Additionally, there are two peaks observed at 399.5eV and 401.5eV from the N1s spectrum (Fig. 4D), which are associated with the CNC and NH bands [27]. These results demonstrate that the as-prepared C-dots contain lots of CO, NH2, and COOH groups on the surfaces, as supported by the above FT-IR analysis. The functional groups endow high selectivity of C-dots for Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection.

The fluorescence quantum yield (Y u) of C-dots in aqueous solution can be calculated using the following equation [28]:
Y u
=
Y s
⋅
F u
F s
⋅
A s
A u
where Y u, F u, and A u are the fluorescence quantum yield, integrated area of emission, and the absorbance at the excited wavelength of the sample, respectively. And Y s, F s, and A s are the fluorescence quantum yield, integrated area of emission, and the absorbance at the excited wavelength of the reference, respectively. The photoluminescence quantum yield of the as-obtained C-dots was calculated to be 50.78%, using quinine sulfate as a reference [29,30]. This value is higher than those of C-dots reported previously (11.5%, 6.3%, 6.9%) [18,23,24].

As illustrated in Fig. 5A, changing the pH values from 4 to 8 in HAC–NaAC solutions yields negligible effects on the fluorescence intensities of C-dots. It indicates that C-dots are insensitive to the pH values. It is more stable than C-dots reported before [18]. And the fluorescence intensities are almost constant by storing the sample for 30min, suggesting good stability of C-dots (Fig. 5B). This phenomenon is comparable with C-dots reported in the literature [24,25]. All of these results confirm the efficient synthesis of water-soluble and good stable C-dots with blue photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection for Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ is essential because of their high toxicity to organisms [31]. Herein, the as-prepared C-dots were explored as a sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. Besides, the selectivity of C-dots toward Hg2+ and Fe3+ was also examined (Fig. 6 ) with other 12 metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and Fe2+) as control experiments. The fluorescence intensities of C-dots were greatly decreased in the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+, while the other ions display weak or even negligible effects on the fluorescence intensities. These observations reflect that the fluorescence of C-dots have response to Hg2+ and Fe3+, and thereby can be used as a fluorescent sensor for the assay of Hg2+ and Fe3+.

Fluorescence quenching process refers to fluorescent molecules react with quencher molecules, inducing the decay of the fluorescence intensity [32], mainly via static quenching and/or dynamic quenching. Static quenching refers to the formation of the complexes by combining ground-state fluorescence molecules with quencher molecules, while dynamic quenching means the collision of excited fluorescence quenching molecules with quencher molecules.

Specifically, the lifetime of C-dots is one of the most useful methods to distinguish static quenching from dynamic quenching by measuring the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of quencher molecules. If the two values are almost the same, the quenching process is static quenching. If not, it is dynamic quenching.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the associated fluorescence lifetime is calculated to be 3.84ns without Hg2+ and 3.81ns in the presence of Hg2+ (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). These results indicate that the fluorescence quenching of C-dots by Hg2+ is a static quenching process.

Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence intensities of C-dots upon the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ranging up to 50μM. As the above demonstration (Fig.S1, Supporting Information), the fluorescence quenching of C-dots is static. Thus, high selectivity of C-dots is ascribed to the fact that Hg2+ and Fe3+ have stronger binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics with amino and carboxylic groups on the surfaces of C-dots, compared with the other metal ions [33]. Besides, the redox potentials of Hg2+/Hg+ (Ф =0.91) and Fe3+/Fe2+ (Ф =0.77) are located between the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of C-dots, causing photo-induced electron transfer from CB to the complex states of Hg2+ and Fe3+ [34]. As a result, the fluorescence is quenched.

More interestingly, Fe3+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence of C-dots in Tris–HCl buffer, while almost no quenching is detected in the case of Hg2+ under the same conditions (Fig. 8 ). This is due to the fact that C-dots own a higher affinity with Tris–HCl as compared with Hg2+ during the assay process. This observation is in good agreement with those in the literature [23]. Thus, the detection of Hg2+ was carried out in HAC–NaAC solutions, while the assay of Fe3+ was performed in Tris–HCl solutions. Moreover, the fluorescence of C-dots cannot be quenched in the presence of Fe2+. Therefore, we can eliminate the interference of Fe2+ by reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ with ascorbic acid, making it possible to detect Hg2+ and Fe3+, respectively, as supported by the report before [26].

The effects of pH values on the analysis of Hg2+ were investigated in HAC–NaAC solutions (pH 4∼8, Fig. S2A, Supporting Information) based on the as-obtained C-dots, while Fe3+ is examined in Tris–HCl solutions (pH 4.4∼9.4, Fig. S2B, Supporting Information). Clearly, the quenching efficiency is lower in alkaline or acidic media, owing to the reaction of carboxylic groups at higher pH values and the protonation of amino groups at lower pH values. Therefore, the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ was carried out in neutral media (from pH 7.0 to 7.4), which is similar to the assays in the literature [18,21,22,25,26].

Fig. S3A and B (Supporting Information) display the time-dependent fluorescence spectra of Hg2+ and Fe3+, respectively. We can see that the reaction time of 4min is enough for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ with C-dots. And the fluorescence can almost remain constant within 30min at 440nm. Furthermore, varying the concentrations of NaCl within 500mM have no effects on the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+ (Fig. S4 A and B, Supporting Information), indicating high stability of C-dots toward Hg2+ and Fe3+ even under high ionic strength environment.

Fig. 9 A and B shows the quenching efficiency of C-dots with Hg2+ and Fe3+ by fitting to Stern–Volmer equation, F 0/F =1+ K SV[Q] [25], where K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of analyte (Hg2+/Fe3+), F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 440nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+/Fe3+, respectively.

Fig. 10 A and B illustrate the detection of Hg2+ with two linear ranges of 0.01–0.1μM (I =0.0460+0.8238C, I, the fluorescent intensity, C, concentration of Hg2+, R =0.9900) and 1–100μM (I =0.1030+0.0010C, R =0.9876), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 5.5nM (S/N=3). As listed in Table 1 , these results are comparable with those in the literature.

The detection of Fe3+ is also provided (Fig. 10C and D), in which there are two good linear ranges of 0.025–1μM (I =0.0177+0.0624C, C, the concentration of Fe3+, R =0.9888) and 5–100μM (I =0.1048+0.0010C, R =0.9850), respectively. The LOD is estimated to be 0.075μM (S/N=3). These results are compared with those in the literature (Table 1). Obviously, the LOD of Fe3+ is comparable to those of previous reports, with the associated broad linear range. Thus, the as-developed method is available for the detection of complex samples. Although rhodamine-based fluorescent probes might be more sensitive somewhat compared to the as-prepared C-dots, it is not unfavorable for applications due to their poor water-soluble properties, complicated and time-consuming synthesis processes [36].

In summary, a green and low-cost hydrothermal method was developed for preparation of water-soluble fluorescent C-dots, utilizing Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source. The as-prepared C-dots have a bright blue fluorescence at 440nm and a relatively high quantum yield of 50.78%. The as-synthesized C-dots can be used for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by simply using the buffer solution of HAC–NaAC for Hg2+ and Tris–HCl for Fe3+, which possess the advantages of good selectivity, fast response, low-cost, and broad linear ranges from 0.01 to 100μM for Hg2+ while from 0.025 to 100μM for Fe3+, as well as LOD of 5.5nM (Hg2+) and 0.075μM (Fe3+) (S/N=3). The present strategy provides a new strategy for developing low-cost and selective sensors for detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21275131 and 21275130), Zhejiang Environmental Protection Bureau (No. 2013A025) and The National College Student Innovation Training Program (No. 201410345019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.03.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jing Yu received her bachelor degree in Qingdao University in 2012. She is currently a master candidate in College of Geography and Environmental Science of Zhejiang Normal University. Her research interest is environmental analysis.

Na Song received her bachelor degree from Qingdao University in 2012. She is currently a master candidate in College of Geography and Environmental Science of Zhejiang Normal University. Her research interest is environmental analysis.
Ya-Kun Zhang studies in College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University as a postgraduate student.

Shu-Xian Zhong received her bachelor degree from College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Shandong Normal University in 2008 and her master degree from College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University in 2011. She is currently an analyst in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. Her research interest is spectral analysis and environmental analysis.

Ai-Jun Wang is an associate professor in College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University. He received her Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2007. His research interests include electrochemical sensor, functional nanomaterials and fuel cells.

Jianrong Chen received his BE and ME degrees in Department of Chemistry, from Hangzhou University in 1983 and 1988, respectively. He received his Ph.D. degrees from Singapore Nanyang Technology University. He is now a professor at College of Geography and Environmental Science, of Zhejiang Normal University. His research interests cover spectral analysis and chemical analysis.
